summary: Growing up in Benham, Kentucky, I remember it was like one big family where everybody knew each other. I attended grade school there through the sixth grade until the school consolidated around 1961 or 1962. Benham was more domestic in culture compared to Lynch, and it had its own company store which was fabulous but expensive. Although I didn’t live in the coal camp itself, Benham was a central part of my early childhood and schooling.

I grew up in Clutts, Kentucky, which was a bit different from the typical coal camp experience because it was located between Benham and Lynch. My father had purchased thirteen acres there and built our own home outside of the Tri-Cities area, so I was somewhat isolated from the coal camp community. This upbringing made me feel a bit different from other kids, but it was a good environment filled with respect and love within my immediate family.

After Benham’s school closed around 1961 or 1962, I transferred to Lynch, Kentucky, for better quality education. Lynch was established by U.S. Steel Corporation and had a diverse population including many European immigrants and African Americans from the South. The cultural mix was very different from Benham, and I enjoyed my time there, especially in subjects like mathematics and chemistry. Lynch also had a strong football culture where I learned valuable lessons about teamwork and communication.

My family’s home was located outside of the Tri-Cities area, which set us apart from the typical coal camp living in Benham or Lynch. Living outside the Tri-Cities meant we were more self-sufficient and somewhat isolated from the main coal town communities. This unique setting influenced my early childhood experience and upbringing before I started school.

U.S. Steel Corporation played a big role in the development of Lynch, Kentucky, where I attended school after Benham’s school closed. They actively recruited foreign workers, bringing in many European immigrants and African Americans to work in the mines. This created a culturally diverse environment in Lynch, which influenced the school culture and community. U.S. Steel’s presence was central to the town’s identity and economy during my youth.

I heard stories that U.S. Steel sent representatives to Ellis Island to recruit European immigrants to come work in Lynch, Kentucky. Many of the stonecutters and workers who came to Lynch were from places like Hungary and Poland, and this added to the town’s diverse cultural makeup. Ellis Island was a key point of entry for these workers who helped build the community and contributed their skills, especially in stonework.

Kingdom Come State Park is connected to my memories through John Coppinger’s family, who donated a large amount of land that became the park. I also visited the park area with Bruner Gilliam and his grandson Joe McCauley, hiking up to the fire tower and seeing the Devil’s Rocking Chair, a notable stone formation there. The park holds a special place in my memories of the mountains and local community.

Bruner Gilliam, a mountain man I knew, was remarkable for having read the Encyclopædia Britannica from cover to cover twice. This showed his dedication to learning despite his rugged lifestyle. His knowledge and intellect were impressive and left a lasting impression on me about the value of education and curiosity.

The Devil’s Rocking Chair is a fascinating stone formation located in what is now Kingdom Come State Park. I visited it on hikes with Bruner Gilliam and his grandson Joe McCauley when we climbed up to the fire tower. It’s one of those memorable natural landmarks that connects me to the mountains and the people I grew up with.
cues: hiking with Bruner Gilliam and Joe McCauley

summary: John D. Staley was my chemistry teacher at Lynch High School and he really sparked my interest in chemistry. I remember one day he demonstrated a chemical reaction with beakers that changed color, and that moment made me want to understand why it happened. His teaching made chemistry come alive for me and influenced my academic path significantly.

Mrs. Rider was a strict but very intelligent teacher who taught English and creative writing at Lynch. She was one of my favorite teachers and her classes helped develop my writing skills, which later inspired me to write a book or two. Her influence stayed with me throughout high school and beyond.

John Coppinger was my history teacher and band director at Lynch High School. He was a genius in music and started me on the tenor saxophone, eventually calling me 'his boy' once I got good at it. His family donated land that became Kingdom Come State Park, which connects him to the community beyond just teaching.
cues: Kingdom Come State Park land donation

summary: Bruner Gilliam was a classic East Kentucky mountain man—tall, lean, and strong. One of his hobbies was chasing rattlesnakes in the mountains, especially during mating season, and he was also assigned to the fire tower as a lookout. I remember hiking with him and his grandson Joe McCauley to the fire tower and visiting places like the Devil’s Rocking Chair. He was also known for having read the Encyclopædia Britannica twice, which was quite impressive.

Joe McCauley, or little Joe as we called him, was Bruner Gilliam’s grandson. He often took us hiking up to the fire tower in the mountains, which was one of the greatest experiences of my youth. Through him and Bruner, I got to explore places like Kingdom Come State Park and the Devil’s Rocking Chair, making lasting memories in the mountains.

Ed Miracle was my football coach at Lynch High School, and he was a good coach who taught me a lot about dealing with people. Playing football under him helped me learn communication and teamwork, especially across the diverse cultures in Lynch. Our team went undefeated my senior year, which was a proud achievement for all of us.
